stories.headline: Jeffco Public Schools found in violation of Title IX over transgender student policies
stories.summary:
The U.S. Department of Education's Office for Civil Rights announced Friday that Jefferson County Public Schools violated Title IX by allowing transgender students to access bathrooms, locker rooms and overnight accommodations consistent with their gender identity, and to compete on sports teams matching their gender identity. The nearly nine-month investigation concluded that male students occupy 61 roster positions on girls' sports teams in the district. The office gave the district 10 days to sign a resolution agreement or face enforcement action.

Assistant Secretary for Civil Rights Kimberly Richey said the district denied 'fairness and equality to female students by allowing males into their private facilities, overnight accommodations and athletics.' Among the findings cited was a report from parents of an 11-year-old girl who said they were not notified that their daughter would share a bed with a transgender girl on an overnight school trip.

Jeffco Public Schools pushed back in a statement, insisting that providing equal access to programs does not violate Title IX and that the OCR's interpretation conflicts with a recent U.S. District Court of Colorado decision that examined the same policies. The district noted that prior federal administrations took the opposite view — that Title IX protects transgender students' access to school programs and facilities.

Nadine Bridges, executive director of LGBTQ organization One Colorado, said the claims risk 'turning young people into the center of political fights' at a time when schools should be focused on supporting students. The group Jeffco Kids First, which filed complaints that helped spark the investigation, called the announcement 'immense relief.'

stories.headline: Red Flag Warning in effect Friday for Denver metro as near-record heat and high winds persist
stories.summary:
The National Weather Service issued a Red Flag Warning for Denver, the Interstate 25 corridor and the northeast Colorado plains from 11 a.m. to 8 p.m. Friday, citing dry conditions and wind gusts of up to 50 mph that create critical fire weather conditions. Temperatures in Denver are expected to reach 71 degrees, roughly 16 degrees above average for mid-March and within a few degrees of the all-time record high of 74 degrees set on this date in 2012.

The dangerous fire weather conditions are expected to continue into Saturday, when a Fire Weather Watch is in effect and winds could gust between 40 and 60 mph. Denver International Airport reported 62 delays and seven flight cancellations Friday morning due to wind impacts. Temperatures Saturday could reach 74 degrees before a cold front moves through Saturday evening.

Behind the cold front, snow is expected to develop in the mountains Saturday night, with portions of northern Colorado's high country potentially receiving six to twelve inches through Monday morning. Wind speeds of up to 70 mph are possible in northern Colorado's mountainous regions Saturday evening into Sunday, with forecasters warning of severely limited visibility and potential for extensive tree damage.

After a brief return to winter conditions, temperatures are expected to rebound quickly starting Monday and could reach record-breaking levels by mid-week, forecasters said. Colorado's statewide snowpack stands at 61 percent of normal for the date, a new record low, with a snow-water equivalent of 8.4 inches compared to a long-term norm of 13.8 inches.

stories.headline: Xcel Energy warns of possible power shutoffs in Boulder and Jefferson counties Saturday due to wildfire risk
stories.summary:
Xcel Energy announced Thursday evening it is evaluating whether to implement a public safety power shutoff in parts of Boulder and Jefferson counties on Saturday as powerful winds and dry conditions create extreme wildfire risk. The utility activated enhanced powerline safety settings for five Colorado counties on Thursday as gusts swept across the Front Range.

Andrew Holder, Xcel's director of community relations and government affairs, described the potential shutoff as a 'tool of last resort,' saying the company examines wind speed, relative humidity and ground fuel conditions before making a decision. The company said Saturday's forecast places the region in the top three percent of wind events seen in Colorado.

Jefferson County Emergency Manager Nathan Whittington said his office activated its emergency management team Wednesday evening as a precaution. He warned that any spark or open burning could lead to potentially devastating wildfire, noting that Jefferson County remains under a Stage 1 fire restriction.

Xcel Energy recommended customers in the potentially affected areas prepare for outages on Saturday by charging devices, stocking supplies and making contingency plans. The company said it would provide updates as the forecast becomes clearer ahead of the weekend.

stories.headline: Two students arrested after fake blood, threats prank at Adams City High School deemed 'every parent's worst nightmare'
stories.summary:
Commerce City police arrested two juveniles following what authorities called a highly disturbing prank involving fake blood and threats at Adams City High School on Wednesday. Commerce City Police Chief Darrel Guadnola said the incident pulled valuable law enforcement resources away from real emergencies across the Denver metro area.

Chief Guadnola described the call as 'every parent's worst nightmare' and said the juveniles responsible would face 'tough consequences.' Specific charges had not been publicly detailed as of Friday, but police indicated the students face serious legal repercussions for staging the false threat.

The incident prompted a significant law enforcement response at the school in Commerce City, a community north of Denver in Adams County. School officials have not yet released a statement about any disciplinary measures taken against the students involved.

The case highlights ongoing concerns about school safety hoaxes that strain emergency response systems. Authorities stressed that false threats divert resources from genuine emergencies and carry serious criminal penalties.

stories.headline: Update: RTD directors vote to authorize audit into years of diverted train cars on B and G lines
stories.summary:
Regional Transportation District directors voted unanimously Thursday to authorize the agency's chief audit executive to investigate how train cars meant to serve northwest Denver were diverted to the A-Line between Union Station and Denver International Airport, and to recommend in April whether a full independent audit is warranted. The vote came after RTD CEO Debra Johnson sent a memo confirming the B-Line and G-Line rail service was reduced in April 2020 during the pandemic, and that agency managers ordered contractor Denver Transit Partners in December 2023 to move B and G line cars and operators to the A-Line.

RTD deputy chief operations officer for rail Dave Jensen characterized the move as 'a swap of equipment' that did not require revising the contract, even though the contract obligated Denver Transit Partners to provide the B and G line service. Johnson told directors the agency has no grounds to seek reimbursement because RTD officials instructed the contractor to make the shift.

Director Cindy Arcuri said the investigation should include a review of monthly payments made to Denver Transit Partners during the six-year period of reduced service and whether agency officials previously considered restoring B and G line service. Director Chris Nicholson questioned why service was not restored sooner and what impact restoring it would now have on A-Line performance.

RTD has proposed restoring full commuter train service to northwest metro Denver starting in June, pending board approval. Since April 2020, G-Line trains to Arvada and Wheat Ridge have run every 30 minutes, and B-Line trains to Westminster have run just once per hour.

stories.headline: Lufthansa Group considers expanding service at Denver International Airport amid airline growth push
stories.summary:
Lufthansa Group is evaluating an expansion of service at Denver International Airport as the European airline conglomerate adds 220 aircraft to its fleet and looks to leverage its partnership with United Airlines, which operates one of DIA's largest hubs. The move would represent a significant boost to Denver's international connectivity if realized.

Denver International Airport is already one of the busiest airports in North America, and the addition of expanded Lufthansa Group service could open new long-haul routes connecting Denver passengers to European destinations. Lufthansa Group encompasses several carriers including Lufthansa, Swiss International Air Lines, Austrian Airlines and Brussels Airlines.

The potential expansion comes as DIA is bracing for a strong spring travel season. United Airlines recently forecast record spring break travel volumes through the airport, underscoring Denver's growing importance as a major aviation hub.

Details on specific routes, timing or the scale of any potential expansion were not immediately available. Lufthansa Group's consideration of Denver reflects broader industry trends toward expanding long-haul operations at major interior U.S. hub airports.

stories.headline: Voyager Technologies opens 140,000-square-foot California facility in latest expansion outside Denver
stories.summary:
Denver-based Voyager Technologies has opened a 140,000-square-foot manufacturing facility in Long Beach, California, marking the company's second recent expansion outside of its home base. The space company, which has partnerships with defense technology firm Anduril and Denver-based True Anomaly, has been rapidly scaling operations.

The California facility adds significant production capacity for Voyager, which focuses on spacecraft and space infrastructure manufacturing. The company's growth reflects the broader expansion of Colorado's space industry, which has attracted numerous aerospace and defense-related firms in recent years.

Voyager's partnerships with Anduril and True Anomaly — the latter of which recently opened a 20,000-square-foot manufacturing facility near Boulder — position the company within an emerging Colorado-anchored network of commercial space and defense technology firms.

The Long Beach opening comes as the commercial space sector experiences accelerated investment nationally, with Colorado companies playing an increasingly prominent role in spacecraft development, satellite operations and space domain awareness.

stories.headline: Avalanche rout Kraken 5-1 as Kadri scores first goal in Colorado reunion, MacKinnon closes in on Art Ross
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche defeated the Seattle Kraken 5-1 on Thursday night, with newly reacquired forward Nazem Kadri scoring his first goal since rejoining the team — his first tally for Colorado since his overtime winner in Game 4 of the 2022 Stanley Cup Final. Nathan MacKinnon contributed a goal and three assists, extending his dominance since returning from the Olympic break.

MacKinnon now has points in seven of eight games since the break, totaling four goals and 15 points in that stretch. He sits second in the Art Ross Trophy race with 108 points, two behind Edmonton's Connor McDavid, with Nikita Kucherov third at 106. The Avalanche improved to 8-2-0 in their last 10 games and hold the best record in the NHL.

Goaltender Scott Wedgewood was strong in net again, stopping 28 of 29 shots. He has won all five of his starts since the Olympic break, prompting discussion that he has earned the right to start Game 1 of the playoffs over Mackenzie Blackwood, who has allowed four goals in each of his last two outings.

Kadri, 35, received a standing ovation from the Ball Arena crowd when the team played a tribute video upon his return last weekend. He said the emotional welcome nearly affected his play. The Avalanche next host the Dallas Stars on March 18 in what is shaping up as a marquee regular-season matchup between the two leading Western Conference contenders.

stories.headline: Broncos stand as only NFL team without a free agent signing from another team; fans and media criticize inactivity
stories.summary:
As the first wave of NFL free agency concluded, the Denver Broncos remained the only team in the league not to sign a free agent from another team, drawing sharp criticism from fans and media. The Broncos pursued running back Travis Etienne Jr. but drew the line below $13 million per year; Etienne chose to return to Louisiana. Denver also chased receiver Jalen Nailor before he signed with the Raiders for $23 million guaranteed.

The team has re-signed 17 of its 22 in-house free agents, including running back Jaleel McLaughlin and most recently kicker Jaleel McLaughlin. Notable departures include defensive end John Franklin-Myers, safety P.J. Locke and long-snapper Mike Burton. Offensively, players who took 94 percent of snaps last season are under contract.

Denver Post columnist Troy Renck argued that Broncos Country has legitimate reason for frustration, noting the Chiefs and Chargers both added talent while Denver stood pat despite preseason messaging from owner Greg Penner, general manager George Paton and coach Sean Payton that suggested the team would be active. The inactivity places heavy pressure on the 2026 NFL Draft, with a running back among the top priorities.

The Broncos' rationale includes 11 contract extensions over the past 19 months that reduced their needs, a desire to preserve compensatory draft picks, and confidence in developing younger players. Critics note, however, that coach Payton has yet to develop an offensive skill player into a star during his tenure in Denver.

stories.headline: Two hikers rescued overnight from 14,075-foot Mount Columbia after snow crystals impair climber's vision
stories.summary:
Chaffee County Search and Rescue North conducted an overnight rescue on Mount Columbia on March 7 after two hikers became stranded during a slow descent of the 14,075-foot peak. A call for help was received via emergency GPS service around 9:15 p.m., with one hiker reporting difficulty seeing after snow crystals were blown into their eyes, hampering the descent in nighttime conditions.

Rescue personnel equipped with skis and snowshoes made their way to the hikers as temperatures continued to drop. Contact was made approximately four hours after the initial call, and the rescue team provided assistance for minor injuries before guiding the hiking party off the mountain. The group reached safety at 4:28 a.m.

Chaffee County Search and Rescue North used the incident to highlight key winter hiking safety practices, including the importance of starting ascents early to ensure adequate daylight for a safe return and carrying goggles to protect eyes from windblown snow and ice crystals.

The rescue comes as Colorado's statewide snowpack sits at a record-low 61 percent of normal, though mountain conditions can still be severe in March. Search and rescue teams across Colorado have responded to a steady stream of incidents on high-elevation terrain throughout the winter season.

stories.headline: Denver St. Patrick's Day Parade, zoo exhibitions and regional festivals highlight weekend events across metro
stories.summary:
The 64th annual Denver St. Patrick's Day Parade takes place Saturday morning, beginning at 9:30 a.m. at the corner of 19th and Wynkoop streets near Union Station. The family-friendly event is one of several St. Patrick's Day celebrations happening across the metro area, including festivals in Olde Town Arvada, downtown Greeley and Boulder.

Olde Town Arvada's free St. Patrick's Day festival runs noon to 6 p.m. Saturday with live music, food and artisan vendors. Greeley's 12th annual Blarney on the Block celebration begins at 2 p.m. in the 9th Street Plaza and features a parade, pipe and drum band, Irish dancers and live music. Boulder Social hosts a St. Patrick's Puppy Parade and Costume Contest starting at 11 a.m.

The Denver Zoo Conservation Alliance opened its Glowing Wild after-dark exhibition, featuring 60 illuminated scenes and 175 large-scale lanterns handcrafted by Chinese artisans, running on select nights through May 10. The Bud Light Rocks the Boat free concert series also returns to Steamboat Springs' base area through April 12, with shows starting around 3:30 p.m. on Saturdays.

The weekend's warm temperatures — with Denver forecast to reach the low-to-mid 70s — are expected to draw large crowds to outdoor events, though residents are reminded that Red Flag fire weather conditions remain in effect Saturday with wind gusts of 40 to 60 mph possible across the Front Range.

weather.notable: About 18 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: You know what your problem is? It's that you haven't seen enough movies. All of life's riddles are answered in the movies.

topStory.headline: Army ROTC Instructor Killed in Terrorist Attack at Old Dominion University; ROTC Students Subdue Gunman
topStory.summary:
A shooting at Old Dominion University in Norfolk, Virginia, on Thursday left one person dead and two injured after a gunman opened fire in an ROTC classroom in Constant Hall shortly before 11 a.m. FBI Director Kash Patel and federal authorities described the attack as an act of terrorism. ROTC students in the classroom subdued and killed the attacker; the university sent an active-threat alert at 10:48 a.m. and declared the shooter neutralized by 11:30 a.m.

The victim was identified as Lt. Col. Brandon Shah, 41, a professor of military science and head of ODU's Army ROTC program. A Virginia native and ODU alumnus, Shah was a decorated Army aviator who flew AH-64 Apache attack helicopters in Iraq, Afghanistan, and Eastern Europe, logging more than 1,200 flight hours, including 600 on combat missions. He received two Bronze Stars and an Air Medal with valor, and had grown ODU's ROTC program by nearly 50 percent since joining the university in 2022. Virginia Gov. Abigail Spanberger called him a devoted instructor who "didn't just lead a life of service to our country, he taught and led others to follow that path."

The gunman was identified as Mohamed Bailor Jalloh, 36, a former Virginia Army National Guard combat engineer who served from 2009 to 2015 and left at the junior rank of specialist. According to FBI Special Agent in Charge Dominique Evans, Jalloh entered the classroom, asked whether it was an ROTC class, and opened fire on the instructor when told it was. He shouted "Allahu Akbar" during the attack and was killed by ROTC students in the room; Evans said he was not shot but declined to elaborate on how he was subdued.

Jalloh had a prior federal conviction stemming from a 2016 terrorism case in which he pleaded guilty to attempting to provide material support to ISIS. He had communicated with an overseas ISIS member and, during meetings with an FBI confidential informant, discussed the timing of a potential attack. Prosecutors had sought a 20-year sentence; a judge sentenced him to 11 years in 2017. Bureau of Prisons records show he was released in December 2024 after serving the federally required minimum of 85 percent of his sentence.

The FBI is leading the investigation and has not formally confirmed a final motive, though federal officials have pointed to Jalloh's prior plot to "murder U.S. military personnel" as relevant context. ODU President Brian O. Hemphill praised law enforcement's response and said the university would be closed Friday. Army Secretary Dan Driscoll confirmed that two other U.S. Army personnel were injured in the attack. The episode is expected to prompt scrutiny of federal protocols governing the supervised release and monitoring of individuals convicted of terrorism-related offenses.
